label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's think about how a typical person would answer this question about causation and intentionality. The question is asking whether the man intentionally shot the bird-watcher. The question is about the man's intention. Here's the scenario: 1. The hunter is in a competition and sees a large deer. 2. He knows killing this deer would win him the competition. 3. He gets the deer in his sights to shoot. 4. At the last second, he notices a group of bird-watchers on the other side of the deer. 5. He realizes that if he shoots the deer, the bullet could possibly hit one of the bird-watchers. 6. He doesn't care at all about the bird-watchers; his only goal is to win the competition. 7. He shoots and kills the deer. 8. As expected, the bullet hits one of the bird-watchers. The key question is whether the man "intentionally" shot the bird-watcher. Intentionality in this context would mean that the man had the purpose or desire to shoot the bird-watcher, or at the very least, that he was aware of the near-certainty of hitting a bird-watcher and proceeded anyway with that knowledge. From a typical person's perspective, intentionality generally requires that someone is doing something with the purpose or desire to cause a specific outcome. In this case, the hunter's goal was to kill the deer, not to hit the bird-watcher. He was aware that the bird-watcher could be harmed as a result of shooting the deer, but that was not his goal or intent.
